docs.title: Homelab Infrastructure
docs.text:
Public Disclaimer

This repository and documentation site represent a private, non-public-facing homelab infrastructure. None of the services described here are accessible via the public internet. All connectivity is managed through encrypted, zero-trust mesh networking.

This documentation is provided as a "Build in Public" initiative for educational purposes, sharing architectural patterns and operational insights.

Welcome to the Homelab HQ technical wiki. This project serves as the central documentation hub for a highly automated, containerized homelab environment.

docs.title: 🚀 Vision
docs.text: The goal of this homelab is to provide a robust, enterprise-grade environment for personal services, media, and monitoring, while maintaining a zero-attack surface on the public internet.

docs.title: 🏗️ Architecture at a Glance
docs.text:
Our infrastructure is built on four core pillars:

  1. Zero-Trust Networking: Powered by Tailscale, providing a private, encrypted mesh network.
  2. Internal Reverse Proxy: Nginx Proxy Manager handles SSL/TLS and domain routing within the private network.
  3. Infrastructure as Code: Everything is containerized and managed via docker compose with a unified Makefile API.
  4. Observability: A full Prometheus/Grafana stack for real-time monitoring and alerting.

docs.title: 🛠️ Tech Stack
docs.text:
• Host OS: macOS / Linux (Docker Desktop / Engine)
  • Container Orchestration: Docker Compose
  • VPN / Overlay Network: Tailscale
  • Monitoring: Prometheus, Grafana, Loki, Promtail, Beyla
  • Automation: Apprise, Bridge (custom service)
  • Media: Jellyfin, Servarr Stack

docs.title: 🚦 Quick Start for Maintainers
docs.text:
If you are setting up this lab for the first time:

  1. Initialize Submodules: 
    git submodule update --init --recursive
  2. Environment Setup: 
    cp .envrc.example .envrc
# Edit .envrc with your secrets
direnv allow
  3. Deploy Stack: 
    make up

Read the full Security Model :octicons-arrow-right-24:

docs.title: Security Model
docs.text: The security of this homelab is built on a Zero-Trust Networking Architecture. This means we do not trust any network by default—even our local home network—and we rely on verified identity and encryption for all communications.

docs.title: 🛡️ Core Security Principles
docs.text:
1. Zero Attack Surface: No ports are forwarded on the internet-facing router. All inbound traffic must pass through an encrypted VPN tunnel.
  2. Encrypted Mesh Networking: Every node is part of a Tailscale mesh network, providing end-to-end WireGuard® encryption.
  3. Authenticated Access: Identity-based authentication (via OIDC/SSO in Tailscale) is required for all access.
  4. Least Privilege: Each Docker container runs with limited CPU/Memory resources and restricted network access where possible.

docs.title: 🔒 Network Layer Security (Tailscale)
docs.text:
By using Tailscale, we eliminate the need for traditional VPN servers (like OpenVPN or IPsec) and their associated vulnerabilities.

  • MagicDNS: Internal services are accessed via human-readable hostnames (e.g., grafana.ts.debdut.in) that only resolve within the private mesh.
  • ACL Policies: Tailscale Access Control Lists (ACLs) can restrict which devices can talk to each other, even within the mesh.
  • HTTPS inside the Tunnel: We use Nginx Proxy Manager to provide valid SSL/TLS certificates for internal hostnames, ensuring traffic is encrypted even after it leaves the WireGuard tunnel.

docs.title: 🔑 Secrets Management
docs.text:
We use a "Clean Repository" strategy to ensure that no sensitive data (API keys, passwords, tokens) is ever committed to version control.

  • .envrc (direnv): All environment variables are stored in a local .envrc file, which is ignored by Git.
  • Consolidated Templates: A public .envrc.example provides the structure of required variables without exposing actual values.
  • Docker Secrets (Interpolation): Docker Compose files use ${VARIABLE_NAME} interpolation, ensuring that secrets are only loaded at runtime from the host's environment.

docs.title: 🛡️ Disaster Recovery & Backups
docs.text:
Security isn't just about preventing access—it's also about ensuring data integrity.

  • Nginx Proxy Manager Backups: A hosts_backup.json is maintained to quickly recreate the reverse proxy mapping.
  • Configuration as Code: All service configurations (Prometheus YAML, Grafana Provisioning) are version-controlled, allowing the entire stack to be recreated from scratch in minutes.

Security Audit Status

This infrastructure is under continuous personal review. All dependencies are monitored for vulnerabilities using GitHub's automated security alerts.

docs.title: Internal Reverse Proxy
docs.text: The Nginx Proxy Manager is our centralized gateway for all internal web services, providing domain routing and SSL/TLS termination.

docs.title: 🌉 Role
docs.text:
While Tailscale provides the secure tunnel into the network, Nginx Proxy Manager (NPM) provides the application-level routing.

  • SSL/TLS Termination: Provides HTTPS certificates for internal services using Let's Encrypt and Acme.
  • Internal Domain Routing: Maps internal hostnames (e.g., grafana.ts.debdut.in) to their respective Docker containers and ports.
  • Load Balancing: Can distribute traffic between multiple service instances where needed.

docs.title: 🛠️ Docker Configuration
docs.text:
NPM runs as a container within the homelab stack, listening on ports 80 and 443 of the internal Docker bridge network.

Component Port Description HTTP 80 Redirects to HTTPS. HTTPS 443 Terminates SSL/TLS. Admin Web UI 81 Centralized management dashboard.

docs.title: 🔒 Security Configuration
docs.text:
• Restricted Access: The Admin Web UI is only accessible via Tailscale and is protected by MFA.
  • HTTP/2 Support: Enabled for all internal services to provide high-performance, multiplexed connections.
  • HSTS Policy: Enforced to ensure browsers always use a secure connection for all internal domains.

docs.title: Infrastructure Overview
docs.text: Our infrastructure is designed for low-maintenance, high-reliability operations on a single node.

docs.title: 🧱 The Pillars
docs.text: Pillar Technology Role Networking Tailscale Encrypted mesh VPN and MagicDNS. Orchestration Docker Compose Containerized services and dependency management. Reverse Proxy Nginx Proxy Manager Internal SSL/TLS termination and hostname routing. Monitoring Prometheus/Grafana Real-time observability and alerting.

docs.title: 📁 Directory Structure
docs.text:
The root repository acts as a central orchestrator for all homelab submodules.

homelab/
├── docs/               # (Submodule) This documentation site.
├── monitoring/         # (Submodule) Prometheus, Grafana, Loki stack.
├── jellyfin/           # (Submodule) Media server stack.
├── servarr/            # (Submodule) Media automation stack.
├── tailscale/          # (Submodule) VPN and networking.
├── bridge/             # (Submodule) Custom alerting intermediary.
├── apprise/            # (Submodule) Notification server.
├── nginx-proxy-manager/# (Submodule) Internal reverse proxy.
├── Makefile            # Unified management API.
└── .envrc.example      # Environment variable template.

docs.title: 🛠️ Management Workflow
docs.text:
The Makefile provides a unified CLI for the entire infrastructure:

  • make up: Starts Tailscale first, then infrastructure, then services.
  • make update: Pulls latest images and restarts the entire stack.
  • make status: Shows a combined health status of all homelab containers.
  • make logs: Aggregates logs from all submodules into a single stream.

docs.title: Networking & VPN
docs.text: Our networking strategy is built on Zero-Trust Principles, where all inbound traffic is encrypted and authenticated through a private mesh network.

docs.title: 🕸️ Tailscale
docs.text:
Tailscale is the backbone of our secure networking. It creates an overlay network (Tailnet) that connects all devices—from cloud servers to local Docker containers—without requiring port forwarding.

  • WireGuard® Powered: Every connection is encrypted end-to-end.
  • Identity-based Access: Users authenticate with their OIDC/SSO provider.
  • MagicDNS: Automatically assigns human-readable hostnames to devices (e.g., homelab.ts.debdut.in).

docs.title: 🛠️ Docker Integration
docs.text:
Tailscale runs as a container within the homelab stack, providing a gateway into the internal Docker network.

graph LR subgraph Public Internet U[User on Mobile/Laptop] end subgraph Tailnet U -- WireGuard Tunnel --> T[Tailscale Container] end subgraph Internal Network T -- Route --> NPM[Nginx Proxy Manager] NPM -- HTTP --> S[Service: Grafana] end

docs.title: 🔒 Security Advantages
docs.text:
1. No Port Forwarding: The internet-facing router remains completely closed.
  2. Private Hostnames: Services like prometheus.ts.debdut.in are only resolvable and accessible when connected to the Tailnet.
  3. Encrypted Internode Traffic: Communication between the Tailscale gateway and other services is isolated within the internal Docker network (homelab bridge).

docs.title: Infrastructure Management (Makefile API)
docs.text: The Makefile in the root of the project provides a unified, industry-standard CLI for managing the entire homelab stack.

docs.title: 🚀 Commands
docs.text: Command Role Description make up Start Starts Tailscale first, then infrastructure, then services. make down Stop Gracefully stops all services and infrastructure in reverse order. make restart Reset Performs a full make down followed by make up. make status Health Shows combined health status of all homelab containers. make pull Update Pulls the latest images for all submodules. make update Full Update Performs a make pull followed by make restart. make logs Observability Aggregates logs from all submodules into a single stream. make clean Cleanup Stops all services and removes the homelab network.

docs.title: Start All Services
docs.text: make up

docs.title: Restart a Specific Service
docs.text: make restart glance

docs.title: Follow Logs for All Monitoring
docs.text: make logs monitoring

docs.title: ⚙️ Advanced Features
docs.text:
• Dependency Order: The make up target is designed to start Tailscale and other critical infrastructure before application-level services.
  • Service-Specific Commands: Most commands (like up, down, logs) support an optional service argument for surgical operations.
  • Color-Coded Output: Provides clear, visual feedback on the success or failure of each management step.

docs.title: Troubleshooting Homelab Operations
docs.text: Common issues and their resolutions for the homelab infrastructure.

docs.title: Tailscale Connection Failures
docs.text:
• Symptom: Internal hostnames (grafana.ts.debdut.in) don't resolve.
  • Resolution: Ensure your device is logged into the Tailnet and MagicDNS is enabled in the Tailscale admin console.
  • Check: Run tailscale status in the terminal to verify your connection.

docs.title: Docker Network Conflicts
docs.text:
• Symptom: Containers fail to start with a "network already exists" error.
  • Resolution: Run make clean to stop all services and remove the homelab bridge network.
  • Check: Verify with docker network ls to see if there are any orphan homelab networks.

docs.title: Let's Encrypt Certificate Failures
docs.text:
• Symptom: Nginx Proxy Manager (NPM) fails to renew SSL certificates.
  • Resolution: Ensure that ports 80 and 443 are correctly routed into the NPM container.
  • Check: Verify the NPM logs with make logs nginx-proxy-manager.

docs.title: Monitoring Stack Memory Pressure
docs.text:
• Symptom: Prometheus or Loki containers are frequently OOMKilled.
  • Resolution: Increase the memory limits in monitoring/compose.yaml and restart the stack.
  • Check: Use docker stats to monitor the real-time resource usage of each container.

docs.title: Media Automation Post-Import Failures
docs.text:
• Symptom: mkv2mp4.py scripts fail to run after a download completes.
  • Resolution: Ensure that the Python environment and dependencies are correctly installed in the Servarr stack.
  • Check: Verify the Sonarr/Radarr logs for post-import script execution errors.

docs.title: Automation & Notifications
docs.text: Automation is the glue that connects our infrastructure, media, and monitoring stacks.

docs.title: 🔔 Apprise
docs.text:
Apprise is our central notification server, providing a unified API for sending alerts to over 100+ services.

  • Unified Format: All internal services send notifications to http://apprise:8000/notify/.
  • Telegram Integration: Alerts are routed to a private Telegram bot via the TELEGRAM_URL environment variable.

docs.title: 🌉 Bridge
docs.text:
Bridge is a custom Python service that acts as an intelligent intermediary between our stacks.

Role Description Health Proxy Buffers and debounces healthcheck alerts. Webhook Normalization Converts disparate webhook formats into standard Apprise notifications. Alert Enrichment Adds context (e.g., container uptime) to monitoring alerts.

docs.title: Monitoring Workflow
docs.text:
1. Grafana detects a metric anomaly.
  2. An alert is sent to Apprise.
  3. Apprise delivers the alert to a private Telegram channel.

docs.title: Media Workflow
docs.text:
1. Sonarr completes a download.
  2. A post-import script triggers mkv2mp4.py.
  3. Bridge sends a "Media Ready" notification to Telegram via Apprise.

docs.title: Media & Streaming
docs.text: The media stack is designed for seamless, high-performance streaming across the local and Tailscale networks.

docs.title: 🎞️ Jellyfin
docs.text:
Jellyfin is the centerpiece of the media stack, providing an open-source, private alternative to Plex or Emby.

  • Storage: Media is mounted from a dedicated external volume: /Volumes/homelab/streaming/media.
  • Transcoding: Configured to use hardware acceleration (where available) to support multiple remote streams.
  • Client Support: Fully accessible via standard Jellyfin apps on iOS, Android, and Smart TVs over the Tailscale tunnel.

docs.title: 🎬 Servarr Stack
docs.text:
The Servarr ecosystem automates the entire media lifecycle.

Service Role Sonarr Automated TV show management and tracking. Radarr Automated movie management and tracking. Prowlarr Centralized indexer manager for Sonarr and Radarr. qBittorrent High-performance BitTorrent client with WebUI.

docs.title: ⚙️ Post-Processing
docs.text: A custom Python utility, mkv2mp4.py, handles remuxing of downloaded media: - Remuxing: Losslessly converts MKV to MP4 (copying codecs). - Automation: Triggered via Sonarr/Radarr post-import scripts to ensure maximum compatibility with streaming clients.

docs.title: Monitoring Stack
docs.text: Our monitoring stack provides deep visibility into the health and performance of all homelab services, from hardware metrics to application-level traces.

docs.title: 📊 Overview
docs.text:
The stack is built on the LGTM (Loki, Grafana, Tempo, Mimir) philosophy, optimized for a single-node homelab environment.

Component Role Prometheus Metric collection and time-series database. Grafana Central visualization and alerting dashboard. Loki Log aggregation and indexing. Promtail Log shipping agent for Docker containers. Beyla eBPF-based auto-instrumentation for HTTP/gRPC services. JSON Exporter Scrapes custom APIs (e.g., Speedtest Tracker).

docs.title: 🛠️ Architecture
docs.text: graph TD     subgraph Services         S1[Bridge]         S2[Glance]         S3[Jellyfin]     end      S1 -- Logs --> PT[Promtail]     S2 -- Metrics --> B[Beyla]     S3 -- Metrics --> B      PT -- Push --> L[Loki]     B -- Pull --> P[Prometheus]      L -- Query --> G[Grafana]     P -- Query --> G

docs.title: 🚨 Alerting
docs.text:
Alerts are defined in monitoring/grafana/provisioning/alerting/alerting.yml and are routed via Apprise to Telegram.

  • High CPU/Memory: Triggers if a container exceeds 90 0x0p+0llocation for 5 minutes.
  • Service Down: Triggers if a healthcheck fails for 2 consecutive polls.
